figures.caption: Table 1: LIBERO-Plus benchmark zero-shot performance. Results are shown in success rate (%).

Figure 4: Stage-wise selection probabilities of specialized adapters in the cube catching task. We sample 120 episodes and compute the average adapter selection probabilities for the four stages: drawer opening, cube grasping, cube placing, and drawer closing. Even within the same task, the dominant adapters vary substantially across stages, and each stage mainly activates only one or two experts.

Figure 7: Training curves of the proposed method. Left: optimization trajectories of the total loss, the averaged student EDM loss, and the last-step student EDM loss. Right: trajectory of the load-balancing loss Lbal. The balancing term decreases steadily throughout training, while the overall objective remains stable.
Table 2: Comparison with parameter-efficient adaptation methods on LIBERO-Plus. The column # Parameters denotes the ratio of trainable parameters to the overall parameters.
Table 9: LIBERO simulation benchmark results. Success rates (SR, %) across four LIBERO benchmark task suites [Liu et al., 2023].

Figure 1: Overview of CKT-WAM. Hidden states from a selected intermediate layer of the teacher WAM are transformed by the CKT module. The compressors use learnable-query cross-attention (LQCA) for token compression. A routing module aggregates the relevant branches into a compact context CA, which the student WAM concatenates with its original textual embeddings Et. The input image embeddings are denoted by Ei, and PE is short for parameter-efficient.
Table 6: Parameter breakdown of a single adapter branch. Dimensions: dtea=5120, dstu=2048, db=512, K=32, cross-attention with 8 heads.

Figure 2: Latency-success trade-off across different teacher intermediate layers ℓ∗. The left y-axis reports the zero-shot success rate on LIBERO-Plus, while the right y-axis reports inference latency in seconds per action chunk.

Figure 5: Detailed real-world examples for Clothes Folding and Fruit Sorting. The left three columns show representative intermediate states of the clothes-folding task, while the right two columns show the fruit-sorting task.

Figure 6: Detailed real-world examples for Cube Catching and Unmanned Retail. The left part shows representative snapshots of the cube task, and the right part shows the unmanned-retail task with target beverage selection from a cluttered shelf.

Figure 3: Real-world long-horizon tasks in our evaluation. From left to right: (1) clothes folding, including removing distracting clothes, unfolding the garment, and folding it; (2) fruit sorting, placing the red apple and pear onto the designated plates; (3) cube catching, including opening the drawer, placing the purple block into the box, and closing the drawer; and (4) unmanned retail, picking target drinks from a cluttered shelf. All tasks require completing multiple ordered subgoals.
Table 5: Default experimental settings used in our method unless otherwise specified.
